item.title: [38956] 2026-04 - A-2025-00023 - Canadian Human Rights Commission
item.description: Records for the period January 1, 2019, to October 25, 2025: All communications (emails, memos, briefing notes, minutes) between CHRC and other federal entities (incl. PHAC, Health Canada, TBS, Dept. of Justice) regarding: a. Policy advice or recommendations on the human rights implications of vaccine mandates or digital ID systems (e.g., ArriveCAN, vaccine passports), including privacy, access barriers, or disproportionate impacts on protected groups. All reports, summaries, or evaluations on: a. The volume, outcomes, and dismissal rationales for complaints related to COVID-19 mandates or digital ID enforcement, including patterns of mass filings or systemic findings. b. Statistical data on human rights complaints linked to mandate enforcement (e.g., job terminations, denied services), including data on unresolved or withdrawn complaints. All correspondence (emails, letters, intake forms) between CHRC and external stakeholders (e.g., civil liberties organizations, provinces/territories) concerning: a. Allegations of improper handling of mandate-related complaints, including whistleblower reports or pressures to downplay systemic issues. b. Investigations into digital ID tools' role in discrimination (e.g., data inaccuracies, access denials, privacy breaches under s. 14 of the CHRA). Non-public datasets, dashboards, or statistical summaries tracking: a. Complaint intake and resolution rates for COVID-19-related human rights issues. b. Internal audits or reviews of CHRC's pandemic response, including findings on resource constraints, investigation bias, or handling of complaints. | 50 pages | Source: Canadian Human Rights Commission

item.title: [38956] 2026-04 - A-2025-00013 - Canadian Human Rights Commission
item.description:
Information from the period of January 1, 2010, to March 30, 2025, including: 1. Quantitative Data: Statistical data on human rights complaints filed against major Canadian banks, including but not limited to: a. Banks (Chartered and Major Financial Institutions)
i. Royal Bank of Canada (RBC) ii. Toronto-Dominion Bank (TD) iii. Bank of Montreal (BMO) iv. Canadian Imperial Bank of Commerce (CIBC) v. Bank of Nova Scotia (Scotiabank) vi. National Bank of Canada vii. HSBC Bank Canada viii. Laurentian Bank of Canada ix. Canadian Western Bank x. Manulife Bank of Canada, xi. ICICI Bank of Canada, xii. People’s Bank of Canada, xiii. American Express, xiv. B2B Bank (owned by Laurentian Bank of Canada), xv. Bridgewater Bank, xvi. Canadian Tire Bank, xvii. President’s Choice Bank, Equitable Bank, xviii. VersaBank, xix. Wealth One Bank of Canada, and xx. Home Trust Company (operates as a bank under Home Bank)
b. Online Banks (“Fintech”)
i. Tangerine Bank (a subsidiary of Scotiabank), ii. Simplii Financial (a division of CIBC), iii. EQ Bank, iv. Alterna Bank (subsidiary of Alterna Savings), v. Motusbank (owned by Meridian Credit Union), vi. Oaken Financial (division of Home Trust Company), vii. Motive Financial (formerly Canadian Direct Financial, division of Canadian Western Bank), viii. Wealth One Bank of Canada, and ix. Peoples Bank of Canada (online arm of Peoples Group),
c. This data should be broken down by:
i. Each major bank (horizontal columns)
ii. Protected characteristic(s) alleged in the complaint
1. (e.g., gender identity, gender expression, disability, race, national or ethnic origin, colour, religion, age, sex, sexual orientation, marital status, family status, genetic characteristics, association with a protected characteristic, conviction for an offence for which a pardon has been granted or in respect of which a record suspension has been ordered, or dependence on alcohol or drug, etc.).
iii. Outcome or status of the complaint by activity should be by the following
1. total received complaints, 2. total accepted complaints, 3. total transferred to mediation, 4. settled a. reasonable offer b. other 5. not settled a. discontinued b. assessment c. section 40/41 i. Objection Accepted ii. Objection Rejected iv. Outcome or Status of the complaint by final resolution be by the following v. Dismissal vi. Referred to tribunal vii. Section 40/41 analysis viii. Settled
1. At conciliation 2. At investigation 3. At mediation 4. Pre-tribunal 5. Other ix. Not accepted x. Grand total
d. Referred to the Federal Court—provide the number of settlement agreements that have been enforced.
2. Qualitative Data: Anonymized summaries of the nature and context of each human rights complaint filed against major Canadian banks. These summaries should provide sufficient detail to understand the issues raised, such as:
a. Complaints related to name change procedures; b. Privacy right concerns interesting with human rights c. Instances of misgendering or deadnaming; d. Refusal to accommodate disability; and e. Systemic issues identified in banking services. | 10 pages | Source: Canadian Human Rights Commission
